coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective turn-off-on fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and cysteine using nitrogen doped carbon nanodots derived from citron and urine
coredata.dc:creator: Xavier, S. Stanly John
Siva, G.
Annaraj, J.
Kim, Ae Rhan
Yoo, Dong Jin
kumar, G. Gnana
coredata.dc:description: An environmentally benign and cost efficient cysteine fluorescence sensor is constructed on the basis of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) prepared by using citron fruit extract and human urine waste as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The turn-off-on fluorescence technique is exploited for the sensing of cysteine, in which the initial fluorescence of NCNDs is quenched with Hg2+ via an electron transfer process. Owing to the competitive binding of Hg2+ with cysteine, the ‘turn-off’ fluorescence sensor is switched to the ‘turn-on’ state. Under optimized conditions, NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly demonstrates a lower detection limit and a wide linear range toward cysteine detection. Furthermore, NCNDs display the discriminative detection of cysteine from the similar bioactive molecules and the co-existence of interference species does not influence the detection of NCNDs toward the interest of an analyte. Furthermore, the practical application of NCNDs based fluorescence sensor is validated with the good recovery in human urine samples, which explores the new avenues in the fluorescence sensing of biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Hydrothermal carbonization
Nitrogen doping
Turn-off-on sensing
Urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characterizations of NCNDs
Influence of ionic strength and pH towards the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India Major Project Grant No. MRPMAJORCHEM- 2013-36681 . This work was supported by grants from the Medical Research Center Program (NRF-2017R1A5A2015061) through the National Research Foundation (NRF) , funded by the Korean government (MSIP). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of (i) NCNDs, (ii) NCNDs-Hg2+ and (iii) NCNDs-Hg2+-cystein, and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of NCNDs as a function of excitation wavelength ranging from 265 to 455 nm.
(a and b) TEM images (Insets (b): (i) FFT and (ii) SAED patterns), (c) XRD pattern, and (d) XPS spectrum of NCNDs.
(a) FT-IR and (b) Raman spectra of (i) NCNDs, (ii) NCNDs- Hg2+, and (iii) NCNDs-Hg2+-cystein.

The influences of (a) pH (Inset: calibration plot of fluorescence intensity and pH), and (b) NaCl concentration toward the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs (Inset: calibration plot of fluorescence intensity and NaCl concentration).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCNDs at various concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 1 to 285 μM (Inset a: calibration plot of fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ ions concentration), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of NCNDs in the presence of 285 μM concentration of various interference metal ions and Hg2+ ions and (c) corresponding fluorescence emission intensity profile, and (d) competitive effects of 285 μM various interference metal ions toward the detection of Hg2+ ions (F0 and F represent, respectively, the fluorescence intensities of NCNDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly at various concentrations of cysteine ranging from 0 to 25 μM (Inset (a): calibration plot of fluorescence intensity and cysteine concentration), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly in the presence of 100 μM concentration of various interference biomolecules and 25 μM cysteine and (c) corresponding fluorescence emission intensity profile, and (d) competitive effect of 100 μM various interference biomolecules toward the detection of cysteine (F0 and F, represent, respectively, the fluorescence intensities of NCNDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions).

Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of (a) Hg2+ ions in NCNDs and (b) cysteine in NCNDs/Hg2+ spiked in urine samples, and (c) fluorescence decay curves of studied nanostructures as a function of time (λex = 350 nm; λem = 444 nm).

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective turn-off-on fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and cysteine using nitrogen doped carbon nanodots derived from citron and urine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625021, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625021, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: R&D Center for CANUTECH, Business Incubation Center and Department of Bioenvironmental Chemistry, Chonbuk National University, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Business Incubation Center and Department of Bioenvironmental Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science, Department of Energy Storage/Conversion Engineering of Graduate School, and Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Research Center, Chonbuk National University, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Storage/Conversion Engineering of Graduate School, and Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An environmentally benign and cost efficient cysteine fluorescence sensor is constructed on the basis of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) prepared by using citron fruit extract and human urine waste as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The turn-off-on fluorescence technique is exploited for the sensing of cysteine, in which the initial fluorescence of NCNDs is quenched with Hg2+ via an electron transfer process. Owing to the competitive binding of Hg2+ with cysteine, the ‘turn-off’ fluorescence sensor is switched to the ‘turn-on’ state. Under optimized conditions, NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly demonstrates a lower detection limit and a wide linear range toward cysteine detection. Furthermore, NCNDs display the discriminative detection of cysteine from the similar bioactive molecules and the co-existence of interference species does not influence the detection of NCNDs toward the interest of an analyte. Furthermore, the practical application of NCNDs based fluorescence sensor is validated with the good recovery in human urine samples, which explores the new avenues in the fluorescence sensing of biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine has gained tremendous attention as an essential aminoacid for the pivotal biological functions of physiological system including the reversible redox reaction, cellular detoxification, protein synthesis, and metabolism [1]. The anomalous levels of cysteine in human system lead to a number of hazardous syndromes including lethargy, liver damage, edema, skin lesion, brain damage, brain ischemia, Alzheimer, and cardiovascular diseases [2]. Henceforth, the indispensable regulation of cysteine level in human physiological system is highly significant, which could be achieved with the quantitative and qualitative monitoring of cysteine. Currently, few analytical techniques including electrochemical [3], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], capillary electrophoresis [5], and mass spectrometry [6] are available for the detection of cysteine. Nevertheless, these techniques exhibited certain constrains such as complexity, insensitivity, high expenditure, time consumed operation process etc., which restricted their accurate and routine detection of cysteine.

Recently, fluorescence technique is regarded as an enthralling approach for the quantitative and qualitative detection of cysteine, owing to its simplicity, inertness, excellent photostability, superior optical characteristics, wider choice for excitation and emission wavelengths, low detection limit etc. [7]. Although the organic fluorescent probes demonstrated considerable sensitivity toward cysteine detection, the complicated synthesis procedures and its toxic surfaces limited their applications [8,9]. Hence, an immense focus has been paid towards the carbonaceous nanomaterials including graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10], carbon nitride nanosheets [11], GQDs-gold (Au) [12], and carbon dots-silver (Ag) [13] for the fluorescence detection of cysteine. However, most of the efforts are involved with the utilization of expensive metals of inadequate availability and the corresponding sensors demonstrated the delayed response, toxicity, inferior sensitivity, and poor stability, which limited their on-site applications. It conscripted the prerequisites of fluorescence probes such as easier preparation, environmentally benign characteristics, fascinating optical properties, stability in a wide range of pH and ionic strength for the effective and cysteine detection. The aforesaid prerequisites are efficiently satisfied by the carbon nanodots (CNDs) with its distinctive features such as robust chemical inertness, high aqueous solubility, ease of functionalization, affordable cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility etc., which emerged CNDs as an effectual alternative to the existing fluorescence sensor probes [14]. Apart from the profound advantageous features of CNDs, a single effort alone has been achieved on cysteine detection with CNDs-Hg2+ assembly [15] with the poor quantum yield and low photoluminescence behavior of CNDs. Furthermore, the stability of as-prepared CNDs under high ionic strength conditions and competitive studies was not detailed, which conscripted the non-reliability of developed probes in real sample analysis.

Henceforth, the improvement of quantum yield and photoluminescence properties of CNDs is highly essential for high performance cysteine fluorescence sensors, which could be finely tuned with the heteroatom doping processes. The firm valence bonds and equivalent atomic size with carbon construct nitrogen (N) atom as a preferential doping element for tailoring the electronic properties of carbonaceous materials [16,17]. With the above perspective, various N sources such as ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) [15], ethylene diamine [18], dicyandiamide [19], hydrazine [20], and ammonia [21], were utilized for the N-doping of CNDs (NCNDs). From the aforementioned reports, it is clear that the preparation of CNDs and its heteroatoms doping were achieved with the toxic carbon and N sources, which collectively increased the toxicity of as-prepared fluorescence probes that may negatively influence the human health and environment. The core objective of biosensors is to essentially recognize biomolecules for the maintenance of a quality and sustainable healthy environment. Contrarily, if the probe system itself exhibits high toxicity, it would significantly fail in fulfilling the significant intention of a developed sensor system. Accordingly, we report the rational development of environmentally benign NCNDs with the aid of natural resources and address its high performance fluorescence turn-off-on detection towards cysteine under various conditions.

Cysteine (Cys, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99%), arginine (Arg, Sigma Aldrich, 99%), methionine (Met, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), threonine (Thr, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), histidine (His, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99%), alanine (Ala, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98.5%), glycine (Gly, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99.5%), valine (Val, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), tryptophan (Try, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), phenyl alanine (PhAl, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), serine (Ser, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99%), leucine (Leu, Sigma Aldrich, ≥98%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99.9%), copper chloride (CuCl2, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99.9%), aluminium nitrate (Al(NO3)3, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99.9%), chromium chloride (CrCl3, Sigma Aldrich, ≥99.9%), ferrous sulphate (FeSO4, AR, Merck, ≥99.99%), ferric chloride (FeCl3, AR, Merck, ≥99.9%), nickel chloride (NiCl2, AR, Merck, ≥99.9%), cobalt chloride (CoCl2, AR, Merck, ≥99.9%) sodium chloride (NaCl, AR, Merck, ≥99.5%), homocysteine (HoCys, sigma aldrich, ≥98.0%), and glutathione (GSH, sigma aldrich, ≥98.0%) were purchased and used without any further purification.

In a typical synthesis, the pulp free citron fruit was squeezed and the obtained juice was filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtered 15 mL juice was added with 10 mL human urine waste and subjected for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 7 h. After the completion of a reaction, the obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged for the removal of less fluorescent particles. The as-prepared NCNDs were stored at 4 °C for further characterizations. For the comparison, bare CNDs were prepared from pulp free citron fruit extract by adopting the similar procedure without the utilization of human urine.

10 μL NCNDs dispersion (2 mg mL−1) was added into 2 mL de-ionized water and the different concentration of Hg2+ ions ranging from 0 to 285 μM. To evaluate the sensitivity for cysteine detection, various concentrations of cysteine ranging from 0 to 25 μM were gradually added into the NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly and the release of fluorescence intensity was immediately measured at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm. The LOD was calculated by using the equation of LOD = 3σ k−1, in which σ and k are referred, respectively, as the relative standard deviation (RSD) of blank NCNDs sample (n = 10) and slope of a calibration plot.

The morphological and structural properties of NCNDs were analyzed with UV–vis-8453 Agilent spectrometer, JEOL-JEM 2100 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Rigaku X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), HORIBA-LabRAM-HR Raman spectrometer, and Perkin-Elmer Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The photoluminescence measurements were achieved with Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer upon the excitation at 355 nm. The life time measurements were carried out using Horiba fluorolog3TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer and the life time (τ) was measured with the following equation using three exponentials, in which T and B represent, respectively, the time and relative amplitude.
(1) τ = T1B1 + T2B2 + T3B3…/100

The absolute quantum yield (QY) of studied NCNDs were measured in a calibrated integrating sphere with Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) equipped with an integrating sphere under 355 nm excitation from a 450 W xenon lamp.

The cell viability of NCNDs was explored with 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay method and cellular toxicity was verified with a formation of soluble tetrazolium salt into an insoluble formazon by mitochondrial enzymes in living cells. The quantity of obtained formazon is directly correlated to the number of survival cells. The HeLa cells were seeded at 104 cells per well in a 96 well culture plate for 24 h before the addition of assay. The HeLa cells were treated with with diverse NCND’s concentration (from 0.1 to 1 mg mL−1) for 24 h at 37 °C in an incubator under 5% carbon dioxide (CO2) atmosphere. At the end of an experiment, MTT assay (10 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1)) was included and incubated for another 4 h. Finally, the formazon product was dissolved in DMSO and the relevant absorbance was measured at 570 nm with BioTek Elisha Reader. Control experiments were achieved with untreated NCNDs for the calculation of relative percentage of cell viability. The cell viability was estimated with the equation given below:
(2) Cell viability (%) = (ODcells with NCNDs/ODcontrol cells without NCNDs) × 100%

The environmentally benign NCNDs are prepared from the natural citron fruit juice and human urine waste as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, via one-pot hydrothermal technique. The citron fruit juice enriched with citric acid is subjected for a condensation polymerization along with the amino compounds of human urine. The aforementioned reaction yields a carbon-nitrogen polymer like network structure and the prolonged condensation leads to the formation of NCNDs. The photoluminescence properties of as-prepared NCNDs are confirmed from the significant UV–vis absorption band at 333 nm (Fig. 1 a(i)), corresponding with C=O/C-OH functional group’s n-π* transition [22]. Furthermore, the as-prepared NCNDs exhibit a strong blue fluorescence behavior under the excitation of 365 nm (UV lamp irradiation) (Inset: Fig. 1a(i)). The addition of Hg2+ ions in NCNDs decrease the intensity of a characteristic absorption band of NCNDs along with a blue shift (Fig. 1a(ii)), yielding a maximum quenching of fluorescence behavior under the excitation of 365 nm (Inset: Fig. 1a(ii)), which is attributed to the binding of surface functional groups of NCNDs with Hg2+. The addition of cysteine retains the absorption band of NCNDs along with the complete recovery of a fluorescence behavior of NCNDs under the excitation of 365 nm (Fig. 1a(iii) and Inset: Fig. 1a(iii)), owing to the complex formation of Hg2+ with cysteine.

The fluorescence spectra obtained for as-prepared NCNDs as a function of excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. 1b. When the excitation wavelength (λex) is gradually increased from 265 to 355 nm, the intensities of fluorescence emission peaks are increased and concomitantly red-shifted. Under the excitation from 365 to 455 nm, the emission peak is red-shifted along with the decrement in intensities, specifying the multi-emissive nature of NCNDs. The tunable excitation dependant emission of NCNDs is attributed to the quantum confinement effect, distribution of different emissive traps on the surface of CNDs, and intrinsic and multicolor properties of NCNDs [23]. The absolute quantum yield of NCNDs is found to be 34.5%, specifying the strong fluorescence behavior of NCNDs. It is further supported from the bright blue emission behavior of NCNDs under the excitation of 365 nm (UV-lamp irradiation) (Inset: Fig. 1a(i)), which is due to the self-passivation of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on NCNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterizations of NCNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared NCNDs are spherical, mono-dispersed and well separated from each other and the mean particle size of NCNDs is established to be 4 nm (Figs. 2a and b and S1). The HR-TEM image of NCNDs exhibits the parallel graphitic lines with the d-spacing of 0.3 nm (Inset: Fig. 2b(i)), which agrees with the (002) diffraction plane of graphite. The corresponding selected angle electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Inset: Fig. 2b(ii)) displays the blurry ring-like structures, confirming the weak crystalline structure of NCNDs. The d-spacing calculated from the SAED pattern is 0.32 nm, which is well correlated with the d-spacing obtained from the HR-TEM image. Although the CNDs prepared under identical conditions reveal the similar morphology and size as that of NCNDs (Fig. S2a), it exhibits the inferior fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2b) associated with the lower quantum yield of 4.2%. The nitrogen doping on CNDs establishes the new kinds of surface states, facilitating the improved yield of radiative recombination through the efficient trapping of electrons, which necessitates the utilization of NCNDs for further sensing analysis. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) pattern reveals that the composition of NCNDs includes the carbon (45.12 at.%), nitrogen (16.58 at.%) and oxygen (38.30 at.%) elements, specifying that NCNDs are predominantly composed of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups (Fig. S3).

The diffraction pattern of NCNDs exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.7° (Fig. 2c), representing the (002) reflection plane. The lattice spacing of 0.37 nm is found for NCNDs, which is slightly higher than those of a graphite interlayer spacing (0.34 nm), owing to the NCND’s oxygen labile functional groups [24].

The wide scan XPS spectrum of NCNDs reveals the characteristic C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks, respectively, at 286, 401, and 532 eV (Fig. 2d) [25]. The presence of graphitic structure and oxygen containing functional groups in NCNDs is identified, respectively, from the C1s and O1s peaks [26]. The successful doping of nitrogen element in the CNDs is validated from the N1s peak and the obtained results clearly elucidate the composition of prepared NCNDs [27].

The FT-IR spectrum of NCNDs (Fig. 3 a(i)) reveals a characteristic band in the region of 3100–3500 cm−1, matching with the NH/OH stretching vibrations [28]. The intense bands at 1620 and 1638 cm−1 are respective with the CO stretching [29] and OH bending vibrations [30]. The presence of COO- group in NCNDs is ascertained at 1407 cm−1 [31]. The existence of rich hydrophilic COOH and amino groups on the surface of NCNDs improve its dispersibility and stability in water. The N-doping on CNDs is ascertained as of the bands at 1470 and 1576 cm−1, corresponding, respectively, to the stretching and bending vibrations of CN and NH [32,33]. The presence of CC unsaturated bands in NCNDs is confirmed from the characteristic band at 1546 cm−1 [34]. The substantial decrease in intensity along with the broadness of NH vibrations in NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly specify the co-ordination of Hg2+ ions with amine (NH) groups of NCNDs (Fig. 3a(ii)). Furthermore, the intensities of carbonyl stretching vibrations are decreased and the corresponding bands are shifted to a lower wave numbers, specifying the effective coordination of Hg2+ with CO and COO− functional groups NCNDs. The characteristic SH stretching vibrations of bare cysteine at 2553 cm−1 [35] is weakened at NCNDs-Hg2+-cysteine, which confirms the complex formation of cysteine with Hg2+ via thiol functional groups. The quenched FT-IR intensities of aforementioned characteristic functional groups in NCNDs-Hg2+ are increased for NCNDs-Hg2+-cysteine (Fig. 3a(iii)), indicating the retrieval of native NCNDs characteristics via the complex formation of Hg2+ with cysteine.

The Raman spectrum of NCNDs displays the D (sp3-hybridized) and G bands (sp2-hybridized), respectively, at 1336 cm−1 at 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 3b(i)) [36]. The ID/IG ratio of NCNDs is found to be 1.08 and the high ID/IG ratio of NCNDs conscripts a number of structural defects in sp2 carbon lattice [37]. The complex formation of NCNDs with Hg2+ lowers the ID/IG ratio to 0.91 (Fig. 3b(ii)). However, the incorporation of cysteine in CNDs-Hg2+ assembly improves the ID/IG ratio to 0.98, that restores the exposure of N atoms on CNDs (Fig. 3b(iii)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of ionic strength and pH towards the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the influence of pH towards the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs, the emission profile of as-prepared NCNDs was investigated at a wide range of pH ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 4 a). It is evident that an increase in pH from 1.0 to 10.0 escorts to an increment in fluorescence intensity and a further increase in pH yields a gradual decrement in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4a and Inset: Fig. 4a), denoting the fluorescence dependent behavior of NCNDs on pH. The fluorescence intensity of NCNDs is decreased with a decrease of pH value from 6 to 2. Under acidic conditions, the surface carboxylic acid and amine groups of NCNDs are bound with H+ ions, leading to the formation of [NCNDs-H]+. It induces the aggregation of NCNDs [38], resulting the decreased fluorsecence intensity. Under the pH values lower than those of 4, the fluorescence intensity of NCNDs remains nearly identical owing to the proton saturation of both nitrogen and carboxylic carbon sites in NCNDs. On the other hand, the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of NCNDs are gradually deprotonated into carboxylates under alkaline conditions with the pH values of 8–10. It generated the protective shells with the negative charge on NCNDs with the lowering of the non-radiative recombination rate of NCNDs, which enhances the fluorescence intensity [39]. The destruction of functional groups on NCNDs is observed with a further increment of pH from 11 to 12 that slightly lowers the fluorescence intensity of NCNDs [40].

The stability of as-prepared NCNDs under the various ionic strengths was evalauted with the wide concentration of NaCl ranging from 50 to 500 mmol L−1. From Fig. 4b and Inset of Fig. 4b, it is clear that the significant photoluminesent variation is not observed for the as-prepared NCNDs with the various concentration of NaCl, disclosing the robust stability of NCNDs under the high ionic strength conditions.

The analytical performance of NCNDs towards the detection of biologically relevant cysteine was achieved under the neutral conditions and the fluorescence behavior of 25 μM cysteine added NCNDs is provided in Fig. S4. Owing to the weak interaction between the NCNDs and cysteine, the negligible fluorescence quenching is achieved for NCNDs-cysteine assembly (Fig. S4(ii) and Inset: Fig. S4(ii)). Hence, it is clear that the fluorescence sensing of NCNDs towards cysteine still remains a great challenge. In line with the above perspective, a modulation is highly essential to effectively detect cysteine via a fluorescence turn-off-on technique and Hg2+ ions are chosen for the modulation of NCNDs in this report.

The fluorescence behavior of as-prepared NCNDs toward the different concentration of Hg2+ was investigated (Fig. 5 a). As substantiated from the PL spectra, the addition of Hg2+ ions quenches the fluorescence intensity of NCNDs and the fluorescence intensities of NCNDs are gradually decreased with an increase in Hg2+ concentration. When the concentration of Hg2+ ions reaches 285 μM, the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs is completely quenched and demonstrates the fluorescence turn-off detection. Upon the further increment of Hg2+ ions concentration, any apparent variation in the fluorescence quenching of NCNDs is not observed, which directs the utilization of 285 μM Hg2+ ions as the optimal concentration for cysteine detection. The coordination interaction between Hg2+ ions and NCNDs vary the electronic structure of NCNDs and affects the distribution of excitons, which collectively facilitates the non-radiative electron-hole remcobination annihilation via an effectual electron transfer process, leading to an effective fluorescence quenching of NCNDs (Fig. S5) [41]. It is also evident that the fluorescence intensities are linearly scaled with the Hg2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 240 μM (Inset: Fig. 5a) with a low detection limit of 0.15 μM.

To optimize the pH level towards the efficient sensing of Hg2+, the fluorosence sensing experiments were achieved at NCNDs with the different pH levels ranging from 2 to 12 (Fig. S6). A minimum quenching was observed under acidic conditions owing to the weaker interaction of Hg2+ with NCNDs. The protonation of amine and carboxyl groups lowered the functionalities available for the sensing of Hg2+, which limited the fluorescence quenching behavior [42]. Under alkaline conditions, the electrostatic interaction between OH− and Hg2+ lowered the possibilities for the interaction of Hg2+ with NCNDs, limiting the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ [24]. The obtained results clearly enunciate that neutral pH is optimal for the considerable exposure of functional groups for Hg2+ sensing.

The direct exposure of Hg2+ ions in all of its oxidation states generate hazardous impacts in health and environment and is usually exist along with the other environmentally relevant metal ions, which directs the indispensable evaluation of the specific detection of Hg2+ against various interferences. Hence, the selectivity test of as-prepared NCNDs was achieved. It is clearly visualized that Hg2+ effectively quenches the fluorescence behavior of NCNDs, whereas the other metal ions do not exhibit any obvious changes (Fig. 5b and c), owing to their non-specific interaction with the functional groups of NCNDs. The excellent specificity of NCNDs towards Hg2+ in comparison with other metal ions is attributed to the stronger affinity of Hg2+ with the surface carboxylic and amine groups of NCNDs, facilitating the effective coordination of Hg2+ with the lone pair electrons of nitrogen and carbonyl oxygen atoms [43]. Owing to the larger ionic radius of Hg2+, the complex formation between Hg2+ and NCNDs is energetically favorable over the other metal ions [44]. Furthermore, the stability constant between Hg2+and carboxylic groups is reported to be higher than the other metal ions [44]. Hg2+ ions possess the considerable electron-withdrawing tendency with the higher reduction potential (Hg2+ + 2e− → Hg2 2+ (+0.908 V)), which is higher than those of other metal ions, facilitating the non-radiative electron-hole recombination annihilation [45].

Furthermore, the cross reactivity of as-prepared NCNDs was examined in the simultaneous existence of possible interference ions. The competitive test of as-prepared NCNDs to Hg2+ ions was validated with the presence of co-existing substances (Fig. 5d). The co-existence of other metal ions do not influence the quenching effect of Hg2+ with NCNDs as substantiated from the 92% quenching effect of Hg2+ ions, which is similar to the quenching (95%) observes under the absence of co-existing metal ions.

The as-developed NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly serves as an excellent fluorescence turn-off-on probe for the detection of cysteine under the optimized conditions. The fluorescence recovery responses of NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly system were analyzed against cysteine with the different concentration ranging from 0 to 25 μM (Fig. 6 a). The addition of cysteine immediately recovers the quenched fluorescence of NCNDs-Hg2+ and switches the “turn-off” fluorescence state into “turn-on” state. The restoration of fluorescence behavior of NCNDs is occurred within a minute and the corresponding recovery kinetics is fairly fast. The fluorescence recovery of NCNDs is ascribed to the competitive binding of cysteine with Hg2+, forming a stable complex through the thiol and nitrogen functional groups of cysteine. It leads to the detachment of Hg2+ from the surface of NCNDs-Hg2+ system, which establishes the “turn-on” sensing of cysteine and the analogous mechanism is schematically exemplifed in Fig. S5. The gradual enhancement of fluorescence behavior is observed with an increasing concentration of cysteine up to 25 μM, beyond which the significant fluorescence recovery is not observed and 89% of quenched fluorescence is restored with 25 μM cysteine. Furthermore, a linear relationship is established between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of cysteine ranging from 1 to 10 μM (Inset: Fig. 6a) and the LOD of developed system is found to be 40 nM toward cysteine. Upon the incorporation of cysteine in NCNDs-Hg2+, the quantum yield is increased for NCNDs-Hg2+-cysteine, which is almost nearer to the free NCNDs, specifying the exposed sites of NCNDs in the NCNDs-Hg2+-cysteine assembly.

The influence of pH on the sensing of cysteine was also evaluated under the different pH levels (Fig. S7). The protonation of thiol groups in cysteine restricts the interaction opportunities with Hg2+, which hinders the fluorescence recovery of NCNDs at lower pH levels [46]. Under higher alkaline conditions, the recovery of fluorescence behavior of NCNDs is limited, owing to the interaction of numerous OH− groups with Hg2+ [24], which restricted the Hg2+ and cysteine interaction and limits the NCNDs-Hg2+ sensing behavior.

The effectiveness of developed sensor probe is strongly dependent upon its ability of recognition towards the specific sensing of an analyte of interest without the influence of other common and analogous co-existing molecules present in the environmental samples. The fluorescence behavior of NCNDs-Hg2+ system was analyzed with the effect of common biomolecules for the consideration of specificity (Fig. 6b and c). NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly exhibits the negligible fluroscence recovery against the other interferences and contrarily, the remarkable fluorescence receovery is observed with 25 μM cysteine (Fig. 6b and c). The efficient fluorescence recovery of NCNDs achieved with cysteine is attributed to the strong binding affinity of Hg2+ with the thiol group of cysteine. In contrary, the thiol free aminoacids do not demonstrate the significant fluorescence recovery of NCNDs. With the realization on the significance of thiol group, the fluorescence recovery tests were achieved with HoCys and GSH. Although the fluorescence recovery of NCNDs was improved with HoCys and GSH than those of thiol free amino acids, their recovery response is negligible in comparison with cysteine. It is ascribed to the lower pKa value and less steric hindrance of cysteine than those of HoCys and GSH [47].

The competitive experiments of developed NCNDs-Hg2+ system was carried out to investigate the selectivity towards cysteine in the presence of co-existing biomolecules including alanine, arginine, glycine, histidine, leucine, methionine, phenyl alanine, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine, homocysteine, and glutathione at a concentration of 100 μM (Fig. 6d). The obtained results infer that the co-existing biomolecules do not influence the recovery effect of NCNDs-Hg2+ system toward cysteine as supported from 88% recovery effect caused by the mixture of cysteine with co-existing biomolecules, which is similar to the recovery effect of sole cysteine (90%), elucidating the practical applicability of proposed sensor in real time situations.

The practical appropriateness of addressed Hg2+ and cysteine sensor was appraised in human urine samples. The urine derived from a healthy volunteer was diluted with de-ionized water in 1:100 (v/v) ratio. The recovery test was evaluated with the resultant human urine by spiking the standard solutions containing different concentrations of Hg2+ and cysteine in NCNDs and NCNDs-Hg2+, respectively, to ensure the accuracy of developed sensor probe (Fig. 7 a and b). Fluorescence strength behavior is gradually lowered with escalating Hg2+ ions concentration (Fig. 7a) and the recovery obtained for the analyzed samples is in the range of 90.5–108.8% with the RSD of 1.3–2.2 (n = 4). The quenched fluorescence behavior of NCNDs with Hg2+ ions is gradually recovered with the increasing concentration of cysteine (Fig. 7b) and the recovery obtained for the analyzed samples is in the range of 97.08–106.6% with the RSD of 1.02–2.06 (n = 4), which elucidates the reliability of proposed sensor probe in real sample analysis.

To understand the response of fluorescence signals of NCNDs and NCNDs-Hg2+ assemblies towards Hg2+ and cysteine, respectively, the time dependent fluorescence spectra were evaluated as a function of time. The fluorescence intensity of NCNDs is quenched with Hg2+ ions (Fig. S8a and b) and the quenched fluorescence of NCNDs-Hg2+ assembly is restored immediately with the interaction of cysteine (Fig. S8c and d). The results reveal that the fluorescence quenching and restoration reach the constant values within a minute, which are rapid than those of the other reports [30–32], suggesting the potentiality of as-prepared NCNDs in real time sensing of Hg2+ ions and cysteine.

The time correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments were evaluated to study the exciton behavior of NCNDs, NCNDs-Hg2+, and NCNDs-Hg2+-cysteine (Fig. 7c). The average life time of NCNDs is found to be 7.7 ns, which is decreased to 6.8 ns after the reaction with Hg2+. The effective interaction between Hg2+ and carboxylic and amine functional groups of NCNDs facilitates the non-radiative recombination of excitions via the effective electron transfer, denoting the occurrence of a dynamic quenching. However, the addition of cysteine into NCNDs-Hg2+ increases the average life time to 7.1 ns, which specifies that the electron transfer between NCNDs and Hg2+ is reduced due to the complex formation of Hg2+-cysteine.

The cytotoxicity of NCNDs was evaluated with HeLa cells via MTT assay method. The MTT assay was performed with various concentrations of NCNDs ranging from 0.1 to 1 mg mL−1 under the 24 h exposure time. At lower concentrations (0.1 mg mL−1), NCNDs display a minimal reduction in cell viability (92%) to the control. Even under the high concentration (1 mg mL−1), the cell viability reaches 82% and NCNDs exhibit low cytotoxicity against HeLa cells (Fig. S9). Hence, it is concluded that the prepared NCNDs are biocompatible and can be used for biological applications.

In general, arc discharge [48], laser ablation [49], electrochemical [50], and ultrasound/microwave techniques [51] are extensively used for the preparation of CNDs. However, the aforementioned techniques exhibit certain limitations including the usage of specified gases, high energy lasers, multistep procedures, high energy consumption etc., which lowers the usage of CNDs. It is effectively overwhelmed with the simple hydrothermal technique reported in this study. In this report, the easily available and environmentally benign citron fruit juice is used as a carbon source for the preparation of CNDs and the nitrogen doping is achieved with human urine as a source for nitrogen. Intriguingly, the preparation of CNDs and its nitrogen doping are simultaneously achieved. The cost efficient and biocompatible starting materials used for the preparation of NCNDs effectively overcome the utilization of conventional and toxic carbon sources such as citric acid monohydrate [44], tartaric acid [44], and ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salts [52] and carcinogenic nitrogen sources such as urea [53], ethanediamine [44], and polyoxyethylene bis(amine) [54], which are used for the preparation of NCNDs. The prepared NCNDs are used as such in liquid phase for sensing studies, where this solution phase governs the merits of size tunability, narrow size distribution, and inclination of tedious separation processes. Furthermore, the tedious and time consuming dialysis process used for the removal of un-reacted raw materials and by-products [55] are effectively avoided in this preparation protocol, which endorses its futuristic applications at a wide scale. Also, in line with the previous literatures, it is clear that the carbon dots based cysteine detection involved the compulsory usage of expensive precious metal nanoparticles such as gold (Au) [56], and silver (Ag) [13], which engages the multi-step processes and also enhances the production cost of the probe material. In this report, the NCNDs prepared from environmentally benign and easily available carbon and nitrogen sources effectively sensed Hg2+ ions, which will be useful for the effective control of toxic Hg2+ ions in the environment. Upon the incorporation of cysteine into the NCNDs-Hg2+, a complex was formed between Hg2+ and cysteine, which aided the fluorescence recovery of NCNDs and responsible for the detection of cysteine. It is hypothesized that the complex formation of Hg2+ with cysteine may lower the toxicity of bare Hg2+ ions and the further studies are under progress to elucidate the above. Owing to the high quantum yield of NCNDs and the specifically tuned interaction between the host fluorophore and analytes, the proposed system demonstrates the considerable quantum yield, high specificity, and lower limit of detection toward Hg2+ and cysteine, which are superior and comparable over the previous efforts (Table S1). Hence, it is clear that the proposed strategy not only addresses the dual sensing of environmentally important analytes but also provides the futuristic dimensions in controlling the toxicity of an environment.

The water soluble NCNDs are prepared via a facile and one-pot hydrothermal technique using cost efficient citron fruit extract and human urine waste as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. With the exploitation of as-prepared NCNDs, “turn-off-on” fluorescence sensor is developed for cysteine detection. The as-prepared NCNDs demonstrates an excellent selectivity towards the detection of cysteine with the negligible interference effect from other metal ions and biomolecules. The considerable recovery in human urine samples validates the real sample analysis of the developed sensor system. Thus the profound advantages including low cost and bio-compatible approach, quick response, excellent specificity, and turn-on fluorosecence response of the constructed sensor system appeals its augmentation in cysteine fluorescence sensor field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India Major Project Grant No. MRPMAJORCHEM- 2013-36681. This work was supported by grants from the Medical Research Center Program (NRF-2017R1A5A2015061) through the National Research Foundation (NRF) , funded by the Korean government (MSIP).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: S. Stanly John Xavier is pursuing his Ph.D., under the supervision of Dr. G. Gnana kumar, Department of Physical Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. His current research interests focus on synthesis of nanomaterials and its application towards biomolecules detection.

G. Siva obtained M.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India, on 2014. At present, he is pursuing his Ph.D., under the supervision of Dr. G. Gnana kumar, Department of Physical Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. His research interest mainly focuses on the development of active nanocatalysts for the application of environmentally benign fuel cells.

Dr. J. Annaraj obtained Ph.D., degree in Chemistry from Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. At present, he is working as assistant professor in the Department of Material Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. His research interest mainly focuses on Bioinorganic Chemistry, Nano Biomaterials and carbon supported non precious metal and metal oxides nanocomposites for the application of drug delivery and biosensors.

Dr. Ae Rhan Kim received her M.Sc. and Ph.D., degrees on 1989 and 1996, respectively, in chemistry at Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Republic of Korea, focused on Photoaddition Reaction of Quinones. She has continusely worked in the same institute as a Research Professor and lecture Professor since 1997, focused on synthesis and characterization of ion conducting polymer/composite electrolytes for fuel cells and solar cells. And then, she has also worked as a head of R&D Center for CANUTECH, Business Incubation Center of Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Republic of Korea scince 2012.

Prof. Dong Jin Yoo is serving as a Professor in the Department of Life Science at Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Republic of Korea since 2011. He has publihsed more than 130 articles in reputed scientific journals. He is mainly focusing on the development of membranes and nanometric catalysts applicable for fuel cells and sensors.

Dr. G. Gnana kumar is working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physical Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. He has published more than 95 articles in reputed scientific journals and contributed few chapters in books. His main research areas include the design and development of novel electrodes, nanometric electrocatalysts and polymer nanocomposites applicable for number of electrochemical applications including fuel cells, sensors, solar cells, lithium-air battery etc.
